title: NME Visual Direction Split
wikitext:
[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Wed Jun 29, 2011 4:12 pm Reichu wrote: Personally, I wouldn't mind seeing more criticism of the kind that isn't dependent on dissatisfaction over items that may well be addressed in subsequent installments.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Jun 29, 2011 4:29 pm Azathoth wrote: Okay, how's this: Rebuild's artstyle, and actually style in general, is too damn soft and rounded.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Jun 29, 2011 4:39 pm Reichu wrote: When I watched those Rebuild features, I realized that I don't have a huge liking for the way the films liberally throw gradient-curtains everywhere. "Damn, that looked better before they applied the effects..."

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Wed Jun 29, 2011 4:53 pm Reichu wrote: Like how all of the line work is blatantly, inhumanly raster instead of nice, gritty ink? Some of the digital "cel"-work looks passable... at least, not at full res. XO

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Jun 29, 2011 5:35 pm I guess I'm seeing this simply from a point of view of lighting and cinematography, but I rather liked the gradients, as they were used to add a sense of shape and dimension to most of the characters in the films. I don't think this is an issue of "Ugh, why even bother with cel animation anymore?" After all, many modern shows use this effect, so I didn't find it odd or distracting that NME would use it as well. Though I did always have a great respect for the hand-painted cels of the original series, or any series before the age of digital shading. Heck, I have a hand-painted cel from Cowboy Bebop: The Movie hanging on my wall, so I do really love and respect that art form. Just the simple fact that it was hand-painted and photographed in actual film did add a sense of detail and texture that the colors used would always add. (Unless you were friggin' AKIRA , in which case you were probably photographing with a higher film resolution anyway, on top of the tens of paint layers used in cel coloring.) Much of the sense of detail or even just a sense of a more interesting looking picture, was lost when the initial switch to digital shading and coloring began. I've noticed such results in shows such as Azumanga Daioh: The Animation and the original Fullmetal Alchemist show. Without the texture of 16mm film and the tones real paints create, the show, in a sense, looked cheaper and flat, even though the areas that were being colored and shaded were the same. Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood did well to add lighting detail with the use of digital gradients, and that's also how I see the use of lighting and cinematography in Evangelion 2.22 . I don't necessarily see it as gradients for the heck of it, but rather an added detail and texture given to the lighting department that help substitute the texture of hand paint and 16mm or even 35-70mm film grain. And I think something like that would be needed in order to make the picture look detailed when it's projected on a cinema screen the size of an apartment. I guess that's where I "stand" on it. It's one of those things where, if you going to use a process of creating the image, whether it be hand paints or digital coloring, one should use the process to it's fullest. Sure, the lack of gradients would have looked fine on my DVD playing on my 30 inch CRT TV screen. But the gradients and the sense of shape they gave the characters looked really good to me in the theater and on the 40 inch widescreen TV my brother uses for Blu-rays. But what would have looked even better is if they had actually hand-painted the whole thing, using clever lens and lighting tricks while photographing each cel in film, as was done in features like AKIRA or The Secret of NIHM , and not just simply remove digital gradients from digital coloring. It's like the debate in the live-action film community when discussing film vs "digital cinema". Sure 70mm film reads more shades of light and colors. But if you're gonna add a CGI special effect in the film, you'd have to scan it into a computer that can't read all of those lights and colors anyway. So you end up loosing those lights and color every time Peter Jackson cuts to a Fell Beast or a Troll or whatever, and, in the end it really doesn't matter within terms of lighting that he shot those scenes in the colors 70mm film provided him. Yeah, the creatures look great, but you're still loosing those colors and shades in the process. So it wouldn't hurt in that case to just switch to digital cinema (RED Cameras) to shoot your picture so everything blends together better. The only way to avoid it is if the filmmaker were to use practical effects in film and not even resort to the computer for anything, which is a process I also enjoy watching when just as much effort is put into the practical effects as they were the CGI effects. So, in my opinion, if you're gonna use digital coloring, you might as well use the digital gradients to add shape, just as the older hand-painted cels would have used clever lighting techniques in their field. If you're not gonna use digital gradients, you might as well hand paint the thing and use the clever lens and lighting tricks to add that look to your animation. I personally like the hand-painted cels better when they take full advantage of the process to that extent. But I would also be dismayed of a digital process didn't take as much advantage into creating the movie's final image as the hand-painted cel process would have taken.

[]:             Quote Post by Nonoriri » Wed Jun 29, 2011 7:17 pm I think I mentioned this in the previous Rebuild criticism thread but because Takeshi Honda and Mitsuo Iso had an apparent falling out during the production of Denno Coil (over Iso's perfectionism) it is unlikely that he will work on NME in any capacity. I don't believe I've seen a fully credible source for this particular event so perhaps it isn't true. I'll say this though, Iso is without a doubt the one of the greatest animators in Japanese animation history. Him not returning to NME after his legendary Asuka v MPE cuts in End of Evangelion (which as a whole is host to some of the best in the industry:http://www.dailymotion.com/video/xywf0_evangelion-key-animator-list_creation, the film itself being a timeless visual masterpiece) is a complete and utter shame. Rebuild is also host to a number of visual oddities, Tsurumaki and Masayuki previously abuse the soft-focus cg blur effect in Diebuster and it makes a really annoying return in NME. Just look at how Sushio's great animation is blurred to hell and back in 2.0: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RA6VKtAPjBs totally washed out and the detail destroyed. Apparently this is an easy effect to utilize and at least to my eyes really undermines the strong mechanical animation in these films. Takeshi Honda himself is a fantastic mechanical animator and his mechanical animation direction ensures that NME has great mechanical animation (which is really the films only true strong point in my opinion) of course I'm not a fan of the CG integration because it is designed to stand out from the traditional animation rather than seamlessly blend into the traditional animation. (Like Sushio/Sanzigen's CG in Panty and Stocking is almost imperceptibly CG) Hidenori Matsubara's animation character designs (probably anyway) are really weak looking and just like how the soft focus blur might undermine otherwise excellent mechanical animation, Matsubara's animation direction also weakens the otherwise good character animation. It doesn't feel like I'm watching a movie-level rendition of these characters even though there is some subtle and nicely characterizing bits of animation and some energetic moments (Toji at the aquarium) as well. The animation director being the one that corrects key animation to look "on-model" i.e they maintain the animation director's uniform character design. As I said before and reiterated in the scans thread, the best use of Sadamoto's designs is with liberal interpretation, like evidenced in FLCL, Summer Wars and TokiKake. (The latter two also have much more natural looking skin tones for characters than NME) Also the talents of the storyboarding comes through in the framing of the mechanical animation, each cut is really visceral and intense. (Though I agree with Azathoth in that Higuchi's original storyboards make that scene a hell of a lot more interesting and that the final product is almost terribly boring and a total waste in comparison) It only serves to widen the gap between how middling the characters look and how good the "set pieces" or mechanical animation looks. The effects animation in NME is also great, Takashi Hashimoto's effects animation is very, very complex and fantastic looking. One of NME's visual highlights. @Azathoth: "Depends what specifically you mean by animation. Certainly there is more actual animation, less "oh shit guise we don't have time to finish this episode, quick crop his mouth out of the shot", greater adherence to character and mecha model, and, in the immortal words of Tolkien, there's a good deal of "incantations, blue lights, and some irrelevant magic" - but I have yet to see any animation work in NME which awes me the way the way the MPE battle awed me - or even the way the 01/02 battle in ep24 awed me." This is because none of the people who worked on that particular sequence have been involved in NME to nearly as significant an extent and in many cases are not involved at all. I'd also like to note that Tetsuya Nishio's cuts (Mari in Eva Unit 02) are quite good as well.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Jun 29, 2011 8:17 pm That's a lot of stuff to take in. Thanks for the information, guys. (I wonder if we could even have a separate topic about this...) Out of curiosity, how do the compilers of those videos find out who is responsible for particular bits of animation? My eyebrow went up when I saw a cut in the Sushio video that was attributed to Honda in CRC. (The one where Sho is just standing there with her glowy mouth open and lights flashing all around her.) ...And I had a suspicion all along that the best-looking parts of EoE's big bitchy brawl were done by Yoshinari. On the other hand, it's a little disconcerting to know that some of the dodgiest-looking parts (in terms of how the Evas are drawn) were done by an animator legend (Mitsuo).

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Jun 29, 2011 8:18 pm Well, focus shifts aren't a CGI thing in the slightest. AKIRA used those to great extent and to achieve a great effect. A director wanting the viewer to focus on one thing on the screen is natural. Nonoriri wrote: Hironori Matsubara's animation character designs (probably anyway) are really weak looking and just like how the soft focus blur might undermine otherwise excellent mechanical animation, Matsubara's animation direction also weakens the otherwise good character animation. It doesn't feel like I'm watching a movie-level rendition of these characters even though there is some subtle and nicely characterizing bits of animation and some energetic moments (Toji at the aquarium) as well.

[]:             Quote Post by Nonoriri » Wed Jun 29, 2011 8:29 pm Reichu wrote: That's a lot of stuff to take in. Thanks for the information, guys. (I wonder if we could even have a separate topic about this...) Out of curiosity, how do the compilers of those videos find out who is responsible for particular bits of animation? My eyebrow went up when I saw a cut in the Sushio video that was attributed to Honda in CRC. (The one where Sho is just standing there with her glowy mouth open and lights flashing all around her.) ...And I had a suspicion all along that the best-looking parts of EoE's big bitchy brawl were done by Yoshinari. On the other hand, it's a little disconcerting to know that some of the dodgiest-looking parts (in terms of how the Evas are drawn) were done by an animator legend.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Wed Jun 29, 2011 8:29 pm Reichu wrote: Well, no... but there remains the ever-looming and unavoidable problem that the most popular targets of criticism lead everyone to the towering, adamantium wall of the Big Wait. All the people involved in these conversations can really do, then, is chase their tails in restlessness, bash their heads against the wall repeatedly, point out that the barrier exists, or meditate quietly.

[]:             Quote Post by Nonoriri » Wed Jun 29, 2011 8:38 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Well, focus shifts aren't a CGI thing in the slightest. AKIRA used those to great extent and to achieve a great effect. A director wanting the viewer to focus on one thing on the screen is natural. I think this is probably due to budget. Most other Japanese animated feature films don't have that level of effects animation that NME has, so they're able to toss more cash and productivity toward character animation. Remember, the film only grossed about $5,306,008 in U.S. currency on its opening day in Japan, mainly due to the fact that only the people who live in a country about the size of the State of California were watching it. Compare that to the more expensive animated films made in the U.S. back in 2009, like UP for example, grossed $68,108,790 U.S. on its opening day, simply because it was released in a larger country. This means that the U.S. studio PIXAR and Disney were able to afford greater detail to both character animation and effects animation knowing that they'd make it all back eventually (their budget being $175,000,000), whereas animation studios in smaller countries like Japan have to split their hairs and make more sacrifices than their American counterparts. (If Khara wanted a budget of $175,000,000 U.S. back in 2009 to cover both effects and character animation, the studio would loose about $169,693,992 U.S. on its opening day, or loose $133,976,996 U.S. after the final worldwide release, and never be able to make anything ever again.)

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Jun 29, 2011 9:01 pm Nonoriri wrote: In all honestly, Akira for example is the most well animated anime because of the talent of its animators combined with strenuous working conditions and a large amount of money (a paltry $30 million) we don't actually know how much money they had to work with for NME, or even End of Evangelion. I'd chalk it up to Matsubara just not being terribly talented over them not having enough money to do it properly, it makes a hell of a lot more sense that way.

[]:             Quote Post by Nonoriri » Wed Jun 29, 2011 9:09 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: It would, but I heard that AKIRA over-blown its budget and took years to make its money back and gain a profit. I could be mistaken though, since I can't seem to get a straight answer from the internet. Though, to be honest, I haven't seen the other works of Matsubara, so I can't comment on his talent outside of the NME films.

[]:             Quote Post by Shogo-Kun » Wed Jun 29, 2011 9:49 pm Nonoriri wrote: Matsubara was the character designer, chief animation director for Ah my goddess and the character designs for the Sakura Wars series.

[]:             Quote Post by Nonoriri » Wed Jun 29, 2011 9:50 pm Shogo-Kun wrote: Really? No wonder they look so good!

[]:             Quote Post by Shogo-Kun » Wed Jun 29, 2011 10:01 pm Nonoriri wrote: Yeah I can understand liking terribly generic things, happens all the time.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Wed Jun 29, 2011 11:02 pm I don't want Rebuild to look like a Ghibli film, I want it to look like Evangelion (but better). Which to me it does. Nonoriri wrote: @Warren: But Anno barely animates for NME at all! and detail in animation isn't incredibly important if anything the constant back and forth between high detail and low detail in NME is a fault rather than the consistently paired down and good looking designs approach taken in Summer Wars.

[]:             Quote Post by Nonoriri » Wed Jun 29, 2011 11:18 pm Warren Peace wrote: I don't want Rebuild to look like a Ghibli film, I want it to look like Evangelion (but better). Which to me it does. I didn't mean that Anno personally animated it, no, just that he set the the general animation philosophy that governs Rebuild. I don't know why you compare it to Summer Wars -- there's some shared talent but it's a completely different film with completely different demands. Certain environments in Rebuild are more detailed than others but I don't see that as a fault. Each environment comes with its own individual designs. Rebuild goes to many different types of places so you're going to get a wide variety.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Jun 30, 2011 10:21 am The NME characters look pretty darn close to the on-model, not quite so outsourced versions of the NGE characters. So the design interpretations themselves in NME were exactly as I expected them to be. I think if there were to be a complaint about character designs and the animation director's interpretation of them, it would best be directed at the original NGE TV series, especially considering the vast similarities between those designs and the visual interpretation of them in both series. But as for the character animation quality itself in 2.22 , yeah it did appear less than stellar. Just what was the budget for 2.22 anyway? And how was it divided into different departments? It would really help gauge Matsubara's talent since his animation work in Ah, My Goddess was for a TV show and therefore created with a TV show's budget. Not About Merchandise Sales  SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by JoeD80 » Thu Jun 30, 2011 1:18 pm Well Anno did animate some of it; he has a credit in key animation. Matsubara is only one of four animation directors on the movie though (Suzuki, Honda, and Okuda being the others), and Sadamoto himself is credited as an assistant animation director.

AKIRA used those to great extent and to achieve a great effect. A director wanting the viewer to focus on one thing on the screen is natural. I think this is probably due to budget. Most other Japanese animated feature films don't have that level of effects animation that NME has, so they're able to toss more cash and productivity toward character animation. Remember, the film only grossed about $5,306,008 in U.S. currency on its opening day in Japan, mainly due to the fact that only the people who live in a country about the size of the State of California were watching it. Compare that to the more expensive animated films made in the U.S. back in 2009, like UP for example, grossed $68,108,790 U.S. on its opening day, simply because it was released in a larger country. This means that the U.S. studio PIXAR and Disney were able to afford greater detail to both character animation and effects animation knowing that they'd make it all back eventually (their budget being $175,000,000), whereas animation studios in smaller countries like Japan have to split their hairs and make more sacrifices than their American counterparts. (If Khara wanted a budget of $175,000,000 U.S. back in 2009 to cover both effects and character animation, the studio would loose about $169,693,992 U.S. on its opening day, or loose $133,976,996 U.S. after the final worldwide release, and never be able to make anything ever again.)
